mappings.label: Qualitative interview
mappings.frame: Frame the interview around a product question — a moment of friction, a task someone is trying to complete, or a decision the team has to make. Tighten the guide to 5–7 questions, run 6–8 sessions in one week.

mappings.label: Ethnography
mappings.frame: Same observational craft — much shorter cycle. Two days in-field per site, three sites, and a one-page synthesis with photos and quotes within a week.

mappings.label: Focus group
mappings.frame: Replace opinion-polling with task-centered prompts. Bring stimuli (sketches, mocks) and have participants react, sort, and prioritize.

mappings.label: IRB / human-subjects review
mappings.frame: Industry doesn't have IRBs but does have privacy and legal reviewers. Write a lightweight consent script and data-handling plan; bring it to legal.

mappings.label: Grounded theory / thematic analysis
mappings.frame: Use the same coding discipline on a much faster cadence. Two analysts, one day, sticky notes (or Dovetail / Miro / Reduct).

mappings.label: Program evaluation
mappings.frame: Pair qualitative signals with product metrics. Define a success metric before launch; instrument it; report the effect with confidence and caveats.

mappings.label: Survey research
mappings.frame: Write tighter items, drop double-barrels, and target a behavior or attitude tied to a feature. Use MaxDiff or Kano if prioritization is the question.

mappings.label: Systematic / literature review
mappings.uxr: Secondary research / desk research / lit review
mappings.frame: Same rigor, narrower scope. Build a one-pager that answers a product question with prior evidence — academic and industry — and notes what's missing.

mappings.label: Teaching / instruction
mappings.frame: Teaching translates to research operations and stakeholder enablement: building self-serve templates, running clinics, and coaching product managers to ask better questions.

mappings.label: Grant / proposal writing
mappings.frame: A research plan is a 1–2 page mini-proposal: question, why now, method, participants, timeline, decisions it will inform, risks. Same muscle, smaller deliverable.

mappings.label: Peer / manuscript review
mappings.frame: Critiques in industry are real-time and verbal. Lead with the decision at stake, the strongest claim, and the weakest evidence. Skip the throat-clearing.

mappings.label: Mixed methods
mappings.frame: Highly transferable. Industry rewards practitioners who can pair n=8 interviews with n=800 surveys or product logs and write the through-line.

mappings.label: Policy analysis
mappings.frame: Policy thinking maps to strategic UX research: framing the problem space, mapping stakeholders, and informing roadmap-level decisions rather than feature-level ones.

mappings.label: Ride-along / shadowing
mappings.frame: Same craft. Shorter window (1–2 weeks), structured artifacts (photos, voice memos), and a debrief interview at the end.

mappings.label: Statistical analysis (regression, R, SPSS, Stata)
mappings.frame: Lean into this. Many UX research teams are starved for someone who can read a regression and pair it with a qualitative finding without losing either.

fallback.frame: Describe it in product terms: what question it answered, who you talked to, how long it took, and what changed because of it. That structure travels.
